skill.name: senior-security
skill.description: Security engineering guidance for threat modeling, security reviews, penetration test planning, cryptography practices, and compliance preparation. Includes reference documentation and simple Python report templates for local analysis workflows.
skill.summary: Plan security reviews, threat models, and audit workflows with local templates.
skill.author: alirezarezvani

security_audit.summary: Manual review dismissed the weak-cryptography, Ruby backtick, reconnaissance, and environment-secret alerts as documentation or generated-report false positives. The only confirmed code behavior is local JSON report writing to caller-supplied paths, and evaluation_output.json contains untrusted self-attestation that should not influence review. No evidence found of network access, credential exfiltration, active exploits, dynamic code execution, or prompt override directives.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Report File Write
medium_findings.description: The three Python scripts accept an output path and write JSON results with open(args.output, "w"). This is a legitimate report feature, but it can overwrite any file the invoking process can write if a user supplies an unsafe path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The write behavior is directly visible in all three scripts and uses a caller-provided path. The risk is bounded because there is no hidden path construction, privilege escalation, or network exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Untrusted Self-Audit Artifact in Skill Package
medium_findings.description: evaluation_output.json declares risk_level safe, safe_to_publish true, and states that all static findings are false positives. This is not an instruction override, but marketplace reviewers should treat it as untrusted package content because it can bias automated or human review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains explicit self-attestation about safety, which matches a prompt-injection warning pattern. Confidence is moderate because it does not include direct instructions to ignore policy, override analysis, or change the evaluator role.

low_findings.title: Environment File Setup Example
low_findings.description: SKILL.md includes a development setup command that copies .env.example to .env. This is standard documentation, not secret access, but users should avoid overwriting an existing environment file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a fenced setup command and does not read, print, or transmit secrets. The only realistic issue is accidental overwrite during manual setup.

low_findings.title: Shell Commands Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The external command findings in SKILL.md are fenced examples for running local scripts, package commands, Docker, and Kubernetes. No file contains Ruby backtick execution or automatic shell invocation by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax appears inside markdown code fences and is not executed by the skill runtime. Targeted review found no subprocess, os.system, popen, or Ruby backtick execution in the scripts.

dangerous_patterns.title: Python File Write with User-Supplied Output Path
dangerous_patterns.description: Pattern: with open(args.output, "w") writes report output to a caller-controlled destination. This should remain documented as a local file-write capability.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is repeated in all executable scripts and directly writes to user-selected paths. It is transparent and local, so the severity remains medium rather than high.

dangerous_patterns.title: Manual Environment File Copy Command
dangerous_patterns.description: Pattern: cp .env.example .env appears as a setup example. This is not credential theft, but it touches a sensitive filename and should be reviewed before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is visible in setup documentation and does not access existing secret values. It can still overwrite local configuration if run carelessly.

content.value_statement: Security reviews are hard to structure consistently across teams and releases. This skill provides local templates and reference guidance for threat modeling, audits, penetration test planning, and compliance preparation.
actual_capabilities: Provides local Python templates for threat modeling, security auditing, and penetration test report output.
Documents security architecture, penetration testing, and cryptography implementation topics.
Validates that a target path exists before creating basic local report data.
Can print simple status reports or write JSON summaries to a user-selected file.
Lists security practices such as input validation, authentication, authorization, and data protection.
limitations: The scripts are skeleton templates and do not perform real vulnerability scanning.
The reference documents are generic and need project-specific security expertise.
The output file option can overwrite files if a risky path is supplied.
It does not include live exploit tooling, network scanning, or automated remediation.

use_cases.title: Structure a Threat Model
use_cases.description: Create a repeatable review flow for identifying assets, trust boundaries, threats, and mitigations.

use_cases.title: Prepare for Security Review
use_cases.description: Use the references to organize code review notes around authentication, authorization, validation, and data protection.

use_cases.title: Collect Audit Evidence
use_cases.description: Generate simple local report summaries and map security decisions to compliance review materials.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Security Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the senior-security skill to create a basic security checklist for my web application. Focus on authentication, authorization, input validation, and data protection.

prompt_templates.title: Threat Model Draft
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the senior-security threat modeling guidance to draft threats and mitigations for this architecture: [describe services, users, data stores, and trust boundaries].

prompt_templates.title: Audit Report Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the senior-security references to outline a security audit report for this repository. Include scope, assumptions, risk categories, and evidence to collect.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Control Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the senior-security skill to evaluate these security controls against my system design. Identify gaps, compensating controls, residual risks, and audit evidence needs.

output_examples.input: Create a threat model outline for an API gateway, user service, and database.
output: Assets: user credentials, session tokens, profile data, audit logs.
Trust boundaries: client to gateway, gateway to service, service to database.
Key threats: token theft, broken authorization, injection, excessive logging.
Mitigations: strong session controls, scoped authorization checks, validation, encrypted storage.

output_examples.input: Prepare a security audit plan for a payment feature.
output: Scope: payment flow, webhook handling, access control, logging, and storage.
Evidence: data flow notes, dependency list, test results, and control owners.
Review focus: secret handling, replay protection, validation, and error behavior.
Deliverable: prioritized findings with clear remediation owners.

output_examples.input: Review cryptography choices for stored customer data.
output: Confirm encryption goals, key ownership, rotation needs, and backup handling.
Prefer maintained platform cryptography and managed key storage.
Avoid custom algorithms, static keys, and unclear recovery processes.
Document residual risk and operational controls.

best_practices: Run the scripts only on paths you intend to review and write reports to a dedicated output directory.
Treat generated findings as planning support and validate every result with human security review.
Customize the generic references with your system architecture, data flows, and compliance requirements.
anti_patterns: Do not treat the skeleton scripts as complete vulnerability scanners.
Do not run penetration testing activities against systems without written authorization.
Do not write report output over existing configuration, secret, or source files.

faq.question: Does this skill run active penetration tests?
faq.answer: No. It provides planning guidance and local report templates. It does not include exploit execution or network scanning.

faq.question: Can it replace a security engineer?
faq.answer: No. It helps structure reviews, but qualified security judgment is still required for risk decisions.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk level medium?
faq.answer: The scripts can write local output files, and the package includes an untrusted self-audit artifact. No malicious behavior was confirmed.

faq.question: Does it access secrets from environment files?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The only environment file reference is a setup example that copies .env.example to .env.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What should I review before publishing?
faq.answer: Review the output file behavior, remove stale evaluation artifacts, and replace generic references with concrete security guidance.
